title: Agentic Marketing Platform — System Design
description: Design the platform that lets marketing agents generate and run campaigns at scale — per-channel content, multi-channel activation, and a closed optimization loop — where the action behind the gate is publishing in the brand's name. On the irreversibility gradient it sits between the Developer and Finance verticals: a bad asset is reputation and brand-safety damage, not money moved or a court filing, but it is still public. It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations and adds two vertical-specific things: a brand-kit grounding corpus and a brand/claim gate (prohibited-claim review plus C2PA provenance for synthetic media), wrapped around a generate → serve → measure → reallocate bandit loop.

nodes.label: Marketer / Brand Owner
nodes.description: Owns brand voice and approves high-risk claims before launch.

nodes.label: Campaign Brief
nodes.description: The campaign request: audience, channels, goal, constraints.

nodes.label: Marketing API
nodes.description: Authenticates briefs, enforces tenant isolation, routes to the orchestrator.

nodes.label: Campaign Orchestrator
nodes.description: Plans the campaign and fans out per-channel asset generation; runs the optimization loop. Submits cleared, approved assets to the delivery workflow rather than calling channels directly, so retries, partial-failure, and publish-attempt state are durable.

nodes.label: Content Agent
nodes.description: LLM/image node that generates per-channel assets grounded in the brand kit.

nodes.label: Inference Backend
nodes.description: Shared LLM/image serving tier (from the Foundations platform).

nodes.label: Brand Kit / DAM
nodes.description: Grounding corpus: brand voice, style, approved assets, and the negative-claim library.

nodes.label: Brand & Claim Reviewer
nodes.description: Deterministic claim/regulatory checks (negative-claim library, required disclosures, blocked phrases) plus model-judged brand-voice conformance with a threshold and evidence.

nodes.label: Delivery Workflow / Queue
nodes.description: Durable publish state machine: holds publish_attempts per provider, enforces generation concurrency, per-tenant spend caps, and per-provider token buckets, and owns retries, backoff, and dead-letter handling.

nodes.label: C2PA Provenance
nodes.description: Stamps generated media with content-authenticity provenance for synthetic-media disclosure.

nodes.label: Draft Asset Store
nodes.description: Pending assets awaiting approval; nothing is published until cleared.

nodes.label: Activation Channels
nodes.description: Ad platforms, email/SMS, social, web/CMS where approved assets are delivered.

nodes.label: CDP / Audience
nodes.description: Customer profiles and segments for personalization at activation.

nodes.label: Performance Analytics
nodes.description: Live engagement/conversion signals feeding the optimization loop.

nodes.label: Bandit Optimizer
nodes.description: Multi-armed bandit that reallocates budget across variants from live performance.

nodes.label: Policy & Compliance
nodes.description: Channel rules (CAN-SPAM/TCPA/GDPR), tenant isolation, and screening of untrusted brief text.

nodes.label: Identity & Token Broker
nodes.description: Scoped, delegated access to channels and the CDP; ties approval to a brand owner.

nodes.label: Audit Record
nodes.description: Append-only record: assets, claim checks, approvals, provenance, channel deliveries.

nodes.label: Observability & Eval
nodes.description: Trajectories, brand-safety and claim-violation rates, campaign performance eval.

functional: Turn a campaign brief into per-channel assets (ad copy, email, social, landing pages, images).
Ground every asset in the brand kit (voice, style, approved assets).
Review assets for prohibited/regulatory claims and brand-voice conformance before launch.
Activate across channels with audience personalization, and optimize live via a bandit loop.
Require human approval for high-risk claims before publishing in the brand's name.
nonFunctional: Brand safety: nothing off-brand or making a prohibited claim is published.
Synthetic media carries C2PA content-authenticity provenance (disclosure).
Channel and regulatory compliance (CAN-SPAM, TCPA, GDPR, template approval).
Tenant isolation; brief and external text treated as untrusted input.
Auditable: every asset, claim check, approval, and delivery is recorded.

capacity.label: Tenants & campaigns
capacity.note: ~5 channels/campaign and ~8 variants/channel => ~40 assets/campaign, ~200k assets generated/day.

capacity.label: Generation cost
capacity.note: Peak ~10 generations/sec; the delivery workflow admits jobs against a concurrency cap (~500) and meters per-tenant token/image budget to bound spend.

capacity.label: Approval queue
capacity.note: Risk-tiered: regulated/claim assets queue for a brand owner (minutes-to-hours latency); low-risk variants flow under policy.

capacity.label: Channel delivery
capacity.note: Each ad/email/SMS provider has its own QPS cap; the delivery workflow levels bursts through per-provider token buckets, owns retry/backoff and dead-letter handling, and tracks each publish_attempt so partial failure across channels is recoverable.

capacity.label: Performance events
capacity.note: Impression/click/conversion events stream into analytics; conversions land late (hours-to-days) within an attribution window.

capacity.label: Bandit cadence
capacity.note: Budget reallocation runs per campaign on a fixed interval over only currently-cleared variants; stale analytics freezes allocation and low-traffic campaigns fall back to a fixed split; regeneration re-enters the gate.

capacity.label: Audit retention
capacity.note: Append-only assets/checks/approvals/deliveries; partitioned by tenant+month, queried by campaign for audit and DSR.

api.description: Submit a brief; the orchestrator generates per-channel assets, reviews claims, and queues them for approval. Idempotent via Idempotency-Key so a retried submit returns the same campaign.
A brand owner approves a specific immutable asset version; the approval snapshots the claim-library version and expires if the library changes. Idempotent via Idempotency-Key.

Submit an approved asset version to the delivery workflow, which opens a publish_attempt, re-checks policy/approval validity, and fans out per-provider deliveries. Idempotent via Idempotency-Key so a retried publish returns the same attempt rather than double-publishing.
Provider delivery callback. Deduped by webhook_dedupe_key and written as an append-only event, so duplicate or out-of-order callbacks are safe.
Emergency kill switch: pause a campaign or a single asset version across all channels and remove its arms from bandit allocation.
Fetch live performance per variant feeding the bandit, with attribution windows applied.

dataModel.name: campaigns
dataModel.note: One campaign per brief, scoped to a tenant; idempotency key dedupes retried submits.

dataModel.name: asset_versions
dataModel.note: Immutable generated asset version with prompt/model provenance; supersession instead of mutation.

dataModel.name: claim_checks
dataModel.note: First-class evidence of a deterministic claim/brand-voice review, tied to library version.

dataModel.name: approvals
dataModel.note: Approval decision snapshotting the exact asset version; expires if the claim library changes.

dataModel.name: publish_attempts
dataModel.note: Durable workflow state for one approved asset version's publish across channels; fans out to per-provider deliveries and survives retries, pause, and partial failure.

dataModel.name: deliveries
dataModel.note: One channel delivery attempt per provider under a publish_attempt; webhook dedupe key makes callbacks idempotent.

dataModel.name: consent
dataModel.note: Per-recipient consent/suppression by channel, region, and purpose, with legal basis and suppression reason; checked at activation. Append-only; a deletion/DSR request tombstones the subject without rewriting audit history.

dataModel.name: channel_policy_versions
dataModel.note: Versioned per-channel/region disclosure and template rules; activation snapshots the policy version it enforced, so a rule change re-checks in-flight publishes.

dataModel.name: performance_events
dataModel.note: Append-only impression/click/conversion events with attribution window.

dataModel.name: bandit_decisions
dataModel.note: Auditable, reversible budget reallocation over only cleared/approved arms.

dataModel.name: audit_record
dataModel.note: Append-only campaign audit across every workflow event.

technologyChoices.concern: Marketing API edge, tenant isolation, and delegated identity
tradeoff: The edge must authenticate the brand owner, enforce tenant scoping, reject unsafe brief payloads, and issue only delegated channel authority after approval.
A managed API/identity stack reduces edge operations, but the domain still needs explicit tenant checks and approval-to-token binding.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed identity and secret stores remove most custom OAuth-token storage, but not the audit trail proving who approved a publish.

technologyChoices.concern: Generation model routing, image generation, and spend admission
tradeoff: Model routing has to choose text/image providers, enforce per-tenant token and image budgets, and cap concurrent generations before provider costs spike.
Self-hosted inference gives cost and data-control leverage at steady load; managed model APIs shorten launch time and absorb model lifecycle work.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed inference endpoint removes GPU fleet management, but the platform still needs admission control, budget accounting, and model/prompt provenance per asset version.

technologyChoices.concern: Brand kit / DAM and approved-asset retrieval
tradeoff: The DAM stores approved assets, voice guidelines, blocked phrases, and negative-claim libraries; every generation must record the exact brand-kit version it used.
Self-hosted vector and object stores give exact per-tenant isolation and version pinning; managed search reduces index operations and retrieval scaling work.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed retrieval removes hand-built embedding and index operations, but not the version pinning that lets a changed claim library re-check pending assets.

tradeoff: Known prohibited claims, required disclosures, and channel rules should be deterministic and versioned; brand voice can be scored by a model judge with evidence and escalation.
A rules engine makes explainable blocks and re-checks easy; managed guardrail services reduce classifier operations but can hide exact policy semantics.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed guardrails can replace some custom classifier hosting, but not the deterministic negative-claim library or the approval policy that gates public publishing.

tradeoff: Decide what is signed: rendered media, prompt/model metadata, asset-version ID, and claim-library version.
Embedding the manifest helps downstream disclosure; storing a canonical copy protects auditability when a channel strips metadata.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed KMS/HSM protects signing keys with less operations work, but provenance still does not prove the asset is safe or compliant.

technologyChoices.concern: Asset-version and approval source of truth
tradeoff: Campaigns, asset versions, claim checks, and approvals need strong identity and immutable version references so a later policy change can expire old approvals.
A relational source of truth keeps version joins simple; a durable event stream or outbox is useful when delivery, audit, and eval consumers must observe every state transition.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A database with conditional writes and CDC/outbox support removes ad hoc duplicate-submit handling across approve and publish flows.

technologyChoices.concern: Generation queue and delivery workflow throttling
tradeoff: Bursty generation and provider rate limits both need leveling; queues and token buckets bound spend, retries, and external API pressure.
A workflow engine makes publish_attempt state, partial success, pause, retry, callback dedupe, and dead-letter replay explicit and auditable.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed workflow engine removes much of the hand-built retry and state-machine bookkeeping for channel publishing.

technologyChoices.concern: Channel connectors, CDP audience reads, and personalization
tradeoff: Activation is connector-heavy: each provider has different idempotency, template approval, rate limits, webhook semantics, and audience-personalization constraints.
Managed integration services speed up common CRM/CDP/channel connectors, but bespoke high-volume providers often still need custom retry and webhook code.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A connector platform can remove some one-off provider clients, but not the delivery workflow that enforces approval, consent, and replay semantics.

technologyChoices.concern: Performance events, attribution, and campaign analytics
tradeoff: Impression/click/conversion volume is high, conversions arrive late, and attribution windows affect which variant gets credit.
A streaming path feeds near-real-time bandit decisions; a warehouse path supports audit, dashboards, and slower campaign analysis.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed streaming plus warehouse sinks remove much of the ingestion plumbing, but not reward definition or delayed-attribution semantics.

tradeoff: The optimizer must allocate only across currently approved arms, enforce budget/frequency/audience caps, freeze on stale analytics, and fall back for low-traffic campaigns.
Feature-flag/config systems make allocation changes fast and reversible; model platforms help retrain and evaluate reward policies.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed experiment/config service removes some rollout plumbing, but not the safety eligibility filter that prevents optimization from escaping the gate.

tradeoff: Consent is scoped by recipient, channel, region, purpose, and legal basis, and must be checked immediately before provider submission.
A policy engine keeps regional disclosure, suppression, DSR tombstones, and template-approval rules versioned and testable.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed messaging suppression removes some opt-out bookkeeping, but DSR/deletion versus retention/audit reconciliation remains domain logic.

technologyChoices.concern: Audit log, observability, and brand-safety evaluation
tradeoff: Audit records need long retention and immutable linkage across assets, checks, approvals, provenance, deliveries, bandit decisions, and pauses.
Observability must track both system health and brand-safety quality: claim-violation rate, off-brand rate, reviewer drift, and stale analytics.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed telemetry reduces collector/storage operations, but not the domain-specific eval gates that block unsafe prompt, model, or reviewer rollouts.

patterns.name: Agent harness (from Foundations)
patterns.what: The deterministic runtime that wraps the model and turns one completion into an agent: it owns the loop, assembles context each turn, parses and executes tool calls, feeds observations back, and enforces stop conditions, retries, compaction, and budgets. This platform consumes the harness from Agentic Platform Foundations rather than reimplementing it.
patterns.whenToUse: The campaign orchestrator runs ON this harness in a hybrid mode — authored orchestration with a bandit/optimization loop and brand/spend approval gates layered on top.

patterns.name: Orchestrator-workers / scatter-gather subagents
patterns.what: The orchestrator fans per-channel asset generation out to content agents.
patterns.whenToUse: Generating many assets across channels and variants.

patterns.name: Human-in-the-loop approval (gate)
patterns.what: A brand owner approves high-risk claims before publishing.
patterns.whenToUse: Before anything goes out in the brand's name.

patterns.name: Bounded autonomy (agent + deterministic gate)
patterns.what: Agents generate and optimize freely but a deterministic claim gate fences what publishes.
patterns.whenToUse: High-volume generation with a brand-safety boundary.

patterns.name: Memory: short-term vs long-term (semantic/episodic/procedural)
patterns.what: Ground every asset in the brand kit / DAM rather than generic model style.
patterns.whenToUse: Enforcing brand voice and reusing approved assets.

patterns.name: LLM-as-judge / trajectory evaluation
patterns.what: Evaluate brand-voice conformance and track claim-violation rates.
patterns.whenToUse: Measuring brand safety and campaign quality.

patterns.name: Prompt-injection defense (CaMeL / Dual-LLM / Plan-then-Execute)
patterns.what: Treat brief text and external content as untrusted; partition from channel authority.
patterns.whenToUse: Briefs and scraped content are attacker-reachable.

patterns.name: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)
patterns.what: Scoped, delegated access to ad platforms and the CDP; approval tied to a brand owner.
patterns.whenToUse: Activating on external channels on the brand's behalf.

patterns.name: Queue-based load leveling; admission control
patterns.what: The delivery workflow levels bursty generation and channel delivery, admitting jobs against concurrency/spend caps and per-provider token buckets.

steps.title: 1. Naive: An Agent That Generates and Publishes (the baseline)
description: Start with the demo: an agent reads a brief and posts copy and images straight to the channels.

It looks productive and is brand-unsafe. Nothing checks the assets against brand voice or the prohibited-claim list, synthetic images go out with no disclosure, and there's no human sign-off on a regulated claim. A bad asset published in the brand's name is public reputation damage.

There is a second, quieter failure too: unbounded generation across channels and variants runs up LLM/image spend and slams each provider's delivery API past its rate limit, with no concurrency cap, per-tenant budget, or queue to absorb the burst.
The rest of the interview adds grounding, a claim gate, approval, and a measured optimization loop.

The campaign orchestrator runs on the agent harness from Agentic Platform Foundations — the runtime that owns the loop, assembles context, and mediates every tool call — so this platform adds the bandit optimization loop and brand/spend approval gates on top of that harness rather than rebuilding the loop.

view.caption: An agent generates and publishes directly to channels with no gate — the brand-unsafe baseline.

steps.decisionPrompt: What's the cost of one off-brand or false-claim asset published automatically?
whyNow: The naive auto-publish makes the brand-safety and disclosure requirements concrete.

concepts.definition: The deterministic runtime that wraps the model and drives the agent loop — context assembly, tool-call parsing/execution, and budget/stop-condition enforcement. Distinct from an eval harness (offline scoring).
concepts.whyItMatters: The campaign orchestrator and its brand/spend approval and bandit optimization gates all run inside this harness, so the gates fence what an agent loop can do rather than replacing the loop.
concepts.example: A campaign request enters the harness, which assembles brand/audience context, runs the scoped agent, and routes spend/creative changes through approval — the bandit loop sits inside the harness.

recap.after: An agent can generate and post campaign assets.
recap.newRisk: Off-brand, false-claim, or undisclosed synthetic content can go public with no review.

traps.trap: Auto-publishing generated assets because they look polished.
traps.why: Polish isn't brand-safety; a prohibited claim or undisclosed deepfake is a public liability.
traps.instead: Ground in the brand kit, review claims, and gate high-risk assets on human approval.

steps.title: 2. Ground in the Brand Kit / DAM
description: The grounding corpus for this vertical is the brand itself: voice and style guidelines, approved assets in the DAM, and the negative-claim library (phrases that must never appear).
Every asset is generated grounded in the brand kit so it sounds like the brand and reuses approved material, scoped per tenant.

view.caption: Assets are generated grounded in the brand kit / DAM (voice, approved assets, negative-claim library).

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you make generated copy reliably sound like this brand and not a generic model?

options.name: Versioned retrieval with citation (recommended)
pros: Each asset records the brand-kit and negative-claim-library version it was generated against.
Approved assets are retrieved by channel/locale/audience and cited as evidence.
A changed negative-claim library can re-flag pending assets by version diff.
cons: Needs a versioned DAM and retrieval index, not just a prompt.
More moving parts than stuffing context.

options.name: Raw prompt context
pros: Simplest: paste brand voice notes into the prompt.
cons: No versioning, no evidence of which guidance was used.
Drifts off-brand as the kit grows past the context window.

concepts.definition: Generating against a brand kit (voice, style, approved assets, prohibited claims) rather than generic model style.
concepts.whyItMatters: Brand voice consistency is the #1 differentiator; ungrounded generation drifts off-brand.
concepts.example: Copy is drafted from the brand voice guide and checked against the negative-claim library.

whyNow: Grounding is the corpus that makes brand-safety review and consistency possible.

recap.after: Assets are grounded in brand voice and approved material, and each records the brand-kit and claim-library version it used — the snapshot a later approval is tied to, so a kit/library change can expire pending approvals.
recap.newRisk: Grounding biases toward on-brand but doesn't guarantee no prohibited claim slips through.

steps.title: 3. The Gate: Brand & Claim Review + C2PA Provenance
description: This is the crux. The reviewer combines two distinct paths: deterministic claim/regulatory checks (the negative-claim library, required disclosures, and blocked phrases enforced as rules) plus model-judged brand-voice conformance with a threshold, evidence, and escalation on low scores. Synthetic media is stamped with C2PA provenance for disclosure. High-risk (regulated/financial/claim) assets require a brand owner's approval; low-risk variants flow under policy.
Nothing publishes in the brand's name until it clears the gate — the reputational analogue of the finance and legal gates, one notch lower on the irreversibility gradient.

view.caption: Every asset is claim-checked and brand-conformance-checked; synthetic media gets C2PA provenance; high-risk needs approval.

steps.decisionPrompt: Which assets can publish automatically and which must a brand owner approve?

options.name: Deterministic rules + model judge + risk-tiered approval (recommended)
pros: Prohibited claims, required disclosures, and blocked phrases are deterministic rules, not prompt hopes.
Brand-voice conformance is a model judge with a threshold, evidence, and escalation on low scores.
High-risk assets route to a brand owner; low-risk flow under policy.
cons: Two review paths (deterministic + heuristic) to maintain.
Voice-judge false positives need a tuning loop.

options.name: Model-only self-check
pros: One prompt asks the model to police its own claims.
cons: A prompt can't guarantee a prohibited claim never appears across thousands of variants.
No deterministic evidence for audit or approval.

patterns: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)

concepts.definition: A maintained list of prohibited or regulated phrases/claims the reviewer enforces as rules.
concepts.whyItMatters: It turns brand/legal risk into a deterministic check instead of hoping the model behaves.
concepts.example: Copy claiming 'guaranteed returns' is flagged and routed to approval or rejected.

whyNow: The gate is what makes high-volume generation safe to publish under the brand.

deepDives.title: The publish-attempt lifecycle is a state machine, not a flag
points: Asset versions are immutable: generated -> claim_checked -> awaiting_approval -> approved -> live, with paused/superseded/rejected as off-ramps; a regenerated variant is a new version that supersedes, never a mutation.

Publishing is its own durable workflow: a publish_attempt (admitted -> policy_rechecked -> submitting -> partially_delivered -> delivered, with paused/failed/dead_letter off-ramps) fans out to one delivery row per provider, so one channel succeeding while another fails is a recoverable partial state, not a lost publish.
create/approve/publish are idempotent (idempotency keys), so retries don't double-publish and a provider accepting a request after a timeout is safe.
Re-check compliance and approval validity immediately before activation: an approval expires if the negative-claim library changed since it was snapshotted.
Provider callbacks are deduped into append-only delivery events, so duplicate or out-of-order webhooks converge; exhausted retries land in a dead-letter for operator attention.
An emergency pause removes an asset version's arms from the bandit and stops new submissions even mid-attempt; rollback reverts to a prior approved version.

recap.after: Claims and brand voice are reviewed; synthetic media is disclosed; high-risk needs approval.
recap.newRisk: Once live, the platform must optimize without drifting off-brand.

traps.trap: Treating brand safety as a model-prompt instruction.
traps.why: A prompt can't guarantee a prohibited claim never appears across thousands of variants.
traps.instead: Enforce the negative-claim library as a deterministic gate before publish.

steps.title: 4. The Optimization Loop: Bandits Over Variants
description: Marketing's distinctive engine is a closed loop: generate variants, serve them, read live performance, reallocate budget with a multi-armed bandit, and regenerate around winners — optimizing while testing.
Crucially, every regenerated variant goes back through the brand/claim gate, so optimization can chase performance without escaping brand safety.

view.caption: A bandit reallocates budget from live performance and regenerates around winners — re-entering the claim gate each time.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you let the bandit chase conversions without ever shipping an unreviewed variant?

options.name: Policy-constrained bandit (recommended)
pros: Allocates only among arms with current cleared claim status and valid approval.
Brand-safety/compliance regressions remove arms from allocation; budget, frequency, and audience caps bound exploration.
Each decision is logged and reversible; delayed conversions use explicit attribution windows.
cons: Eligibility checks add latency to each reallocation.
Cold-start / low-traffic arms need an explicit fallback.

options.name: Unconstrained bandit
cons: Optimizes into borderline claims, audience fatigue, or spend spikes.
Can reallocate to a paused or revoked arm.

options.name: Fixed A/B test
pros: Predictable, easy to reason about and report.
cons: Wastes budget on losers for the whole test window.

concepts.definition: A multi-armed bandit shifts budget toward better-performing variants while still exploring, instead of a fixed A/B split.
concepts.whyItMatters: It optimizes spend continuously, but must stay inside the brand gate to be safe.
concepts.example: A winning subject line gets more budget; a new variant is generated and re-reviewed.

whyNow: The loop is the platform's value; gating regenerated variants keeps it safe.

recap.after: Budget reallocates live to winners; new variants are re-reviewed.
recap.newRisk: Personalization and channels carry their own compliance rules and untrusted data.

traps.trap: Letting the bandit allocate over every variant by performance alone.
traps.why: It will reallocate budget to an arm that has since been paused, revoked, or whose claim status regressed — optimizing into a brand-safety incident.
traps.instead: Restrict eligible arms to those with current cleared status and valid approval; remove an arm the moment its safety metrics regress.

deepDives.title: Bandit guardrails: the loop must fail closed
points: Eligible arms are materialized from current approvals/claim status at decision time, not cached; a paused, superseded, or regressed arm is dropped before allocation, never after.

Reward is conversions within a configured attribution window (not raw clicks); because conversions land late, the bandit discounts arms with too little settled data instead of overfitting a short window.

Exploration is bounded by hard constraints checked before each decision: per-campaign budget pacing, per-recipient frequency caps, and audience/segment exclusion and fairness caps so no segment is over-targeted.
Stale or missing analytics freezes allocation at the last safe split rather than exploring blind; the same applies if the brand-safety/eval feed is unavailable.
Low-traffic and cold-start campaigns fall back to a fixed A/B or rules-based split until each arm has enough settled data to estimate reward.

steps.title: 5. Channel Compliance, Personalization & Audit
description: Activation has rules. Compliance is not one boolean: it is consent scoped by recipient, channel, region, and purpose, with a legal basis (consent / legitimate interest / contract); a versioned channel policy (disclosure and frequency rules); and a provider template that can be pending, approved, or revoked. Email/SMS must honor CAN-SPAM/TCPA (suppression, opt-out), GDPR governs personalization data and deletion/DSR, and personalization pulls from the CDP at activation. Brief text and external content are untrusted input and are partitioned from channel authority.

Every asset, claim check, approval, provenance stamp, and delivery is recorded in an append-only audit, because brand liability stays with the company. A DSR/deletion request tombstones the recipient (suppression_reason = dsr_deletion) without rewriting the audit history that retention obligations require.

view.caption: Cleared assets enter the delivery workflow, which re-checks channel rules (CAN-SPAM/TCPA/GDPR) and approval validity before a delegated channel token activates them; everything is audited.

steps.decisionPrompt: Where do channel-compliance rules live so the bandit can't optimize past them?

concepts.definition: Per-channel legal/platform rules (opt-out, consent, template approval) enforced at activation.
concepts.whyItMatters: Optimization that ignores them creates legal and deliverability liability.
concepts.example: An opted-out recipient is suppressed regardless of what the bandit prefers.

whyNow: Compliance and audit are the floor under the optimization loop.

messages.label: final check (consent+purpose, policy version, template approval, approval validity)

else.label: opted out / approval expired / template revoked

traps.trap: Treating "compliant" as a single boolean checked once.
traps.why: Consent is per recipient, channel, region, and purpose; a user may consent to email but not SMS, and approval can expire when the claim library changes.
traps.instead: Re-check consent, region policy, template approval, and approval validity at activation time, immediately before provider submission.

deepDives.title: Brand liability stays with the company
points: Audit trails are mandatory because the brand owns what publishes.
C2PA provenance supports synthetic-media disclosure obligations.
Treat scraped/competitive content and brief text as untrusted.

Compliance has versioned semantics: consent is scoped by purpose and legal basis, channel policy and provider templates are versioned and can be revoked, and the activation snapshot records which versions it enforced.

DSR/deletion and append-only audit retention conflict only superficially: tombstone the recipient (suppression_reason = dsr_deletion) and drop personal data from the operational consent record, while keeping the de-identified audit events retention requires.

recap.after: Compliance is enforced at activation and everything is audited.
recap.newRisk: Quality and brand-safety must be measured, not assumed.

steps.title: 6. Brand-Safety & Performance Evaluation
description: Measure what matters in two dimensions. Brand safety: track claim-violation and off-brand rates with trajectory eval, so a high-performing variant never hides a prohibited claim. Performance: evaluate campaign lift against goals, closing the loop with the bandit.
This completes the vertical: grounded generation, a brand/claim gate, a safe optimization loop, compliance, and measurement.

view.caption: Brand-safety (claim-violation rate) and performance are evaluated; the loop closes with the bandit.

steps.decisionPrompt: A variant has the best CTR but makes a borderline claim — which signal stops it?

concepts.definition: Measuring claim-violation and off-brand rates across generated assets, not just conversion.
concepts.whyItMatters: Performance metrics hide the brand-safety failure that matters most.
concepts.example: Track flagged-claim rate per campaign alongside lift.

whyNow: Measurement is what keeps the optimization loop safe at scale.

traps.trap: Treating brand-safety eval as a dashboard to watch.
traps.why: Monitoring tells you after the fact; a model, prompt, reviewer, retrieval, or brand-kit change can quietly raise claim-violation or off-brand rates.
traps.instead: Make offline/shadow eval a release gate with concrete numbers — e.g. block a prompt/model/reviewer rollout if shadow eval raises the claim-violation rate above 0.1% or drops the median brand-voice score below 0.8 against the held-out set.

recap.after: Brand safety and performance are both evaluated.
recap.newRisk: Other verticals (sales, HR) gate on deliverability and fairness — see the rest of the series.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Agentic Marketing Platform
finalDesign.description: A brief enters through the marketing API; the campaign orchestrator fans per-channel generation out to content agents grounded in the brand kit / DAM. The reviewer applies deterministic claim/regulatory checks (negative-claim library, required disclosures, blocked phrases) plus model-judged brand-voice conformance; synthetic media is stamped with C2PA provenance. High-risk assets require a brand owner's approval; cleared, approved assets are submitted to a durable delivery workflow that owns publish-attempt state, re-checks policy and approval validity, enforces per-provider token buckets, and handles retries, partial failure, pause, and rollback. It activates on channels with CDP personalization under channel-compliance rules (CAN-SPAM/TCPA/GDPR), re-checking consent and template approval just before each provider submission. A policy-constrained bandit reallocates budget from live analytics over only currently-cleared, approved arms and regenerates around winners — each new variant re-entering the claim gate. Everything is recorded in an append-only audit, and brand-safety (claim-violation rate) and performance are evaluated. Generation, guardrails, identity, and observability are the shared substrate from Agentic Platform Foundations; the gate protects the brand's public reputation.

functional.how: Orchestrator fans generation out to content agents.
Generation grounded in the DAM and voice guide.
Deterministic claim/regulatory checks plus model-judged brand-voice conformance.
Channel activation with a bandit loop.

nonFunctional.how: Negative-claim library enforced as a gate.
C2PA provenance stamped on generated media.
Consent (by recipient, channel, region, purpose), versioned channel policy, and template approval re-checked at activation; CAN-SPAM/TCPA/GDPR enforced before provider submission.
Append-only record of assets, claim checks, approvals, provenance stamps, and deliveries.
Per-tenant scoping of brand kit, consent, and delivery; brief/scraped text partitioned from channel authority.

say: Frame the gate as publishing in the brand's name — reputation damage, between Developer and Finance on the gradient.
It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations.
Draw the agent auto-publishing and list the brand-safety failures.
Ground in the brand kit, then the claim gate plus C2PA provenance and risk-tiered approval.
The bandit loop with re-review, then channel compliance and audit.
Brand-safety eval (claim-violation rate) alongside performance, as a release gate on prompt/model/reviewer rollouts — not just a dashboard.

expectations: Brand-kit grounding and a deterministic claim gate.
Bandit loop with re-review; channel compliance.
Enforces the negative-claim library as a gate, not a prompt.
Keeps optimization inside the brand gate and adds C2PA provenance.
Measures brand safety, not just conversion; builds on the Foundations substrate.
